title: Good Friday
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: March 30, 2018

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

Good Friday falls on March 30, 2018, marking the climax of Holy Week in the Christian calendar. This is the final day of Lent, preceding Easter Sunday (April 1, 2018). The lectionary readings would include Isaiah 52:13-53:12 (the Suffering Servant), Psalm 22, Hebrews 10:16-25, and John 18:1-19:42 (the Passion narrative). The liturgy would feature the Veneration of the Cross and communion from reserved sacrament, with no eucharistic consecration during the Good Friday service.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

In March 2018, the Episcopal Church was in the early stages of preparation for General Convention scheduled for July 2018 in Austin, Texas. The Diocese of Colorado was experiencing stable leadership under Bishop Robert O'Neill, who had been serving since 2008. No major controversies specific to the Colorado diocese were dominating news at this moment, though national discussions around LGBTQ+ inclusion and liturgical modernization were ongoing within the broader Episcopal communion.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver experienced unseasonably warm weather in late March 2018, with temperatures in the 60s-70s Fahrenheit, typical for spring transition in Colorado. The Colorado legislature was in its final weeks of the 2018 session (concluding April 9), with education funding and healthcare debates dominating state politics. No major crises or natural disasters were affecting the Denver metropolitan area during this specific week.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Trump administration was engaged in significant policy debates: the week before included discussions around tariffs and trade wars with China, with announcement of steel and aluminum tariffs creating market volatility. The Mueller investigation into Russian interference in the 2016 election remained a dominant news story. The teacher shortage and education funding debates were prominent national issues following the #RedForEd teacher strikes in Oklahoma and other states earlier in 2018.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Syrian civil war continued with ongoing humanitarian concerns, particularly regarding chemical weapons use by the Assad regime (the U.S. would strike Syria just days later on April 14). The Korean peninsula situation remained tense, though diplomatic developments toward talks between North and South Korea were beginning to emerge. Brexit negotiations between the UK and EU were ongoing, with the March 29 deadline having just passed without a final agreement, extending negotiations.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

America was in a transitional cultural moment of the Trump presidency's second year, with political polarization at high levels and social media discourse intensely divided. The #MeToo movement continued reshaping Hollywood and workplace culture broadly. Culturally, audiences were engaged with Marvel's Black Panther (released in February, still in theaters) and anticipating Avengers: Infinity War (April 27), reflecting escapism into spectacle during a turbulent political climate.
